Let $J$ be the set of cats that can jump, $F$ be the set of cats that can fetch, and $S$ be the set of cats that can spin. We are given the following information:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $|J| = 60$
    \item $|J \cap F| = 25$
    \item $|F \cap S| = 20$
    \item $|S \cap J| = 30$
    \item $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$
    \item Number of cats that can do none = 5
\end{itemize}
We want to find the total number of cats in the center, which is $|J \cup F \cup S|$.
We can use the principle of inclusion-exclusion to find the number of cats that can do at least one trick:
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$
We are given $|J| = 60$, $|J \cap F| = 25$, $|J \cap S| = 30$, $|F \cap S| = 20$, and $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$.
We need to find $|F|$ and $|S|$.
We know that $|F \cap S| = 20$ and $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$.
We can find $|F \setminus (J \cup S)| = |F| - |F \cap J| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$.
We can find $|S \setminus (J \cup F)| = |S| - |S \cap J| - |S \cap F| + |J \cap F \cap S|$.
We are given that 15 cats can do all three tricks.
Let $N(J)$ be the number of cats that can only jump, $N(F)$ be the number of cats that can only fetch, and $N(S)$ be the number of cats that can only spin.
Let $N(J \cap F)$ be the number of cats that can only jump and fetch, $N(J \cap S)$ be the number of cats that can only jump and spin, and $N(F \cap S)$ be the number of cats that can only fetch and spin.
Let $N(J \cap F \cap S)$ be the number of cats that can do all three tricks.
We have:
$|J| = N(J) + N(J \cap F) + N(J \cap S) + N(J \cap F \cap S)$
$|F| = N(F) + N(J \cap F) + N(F \cap S) + N(J \cap F \cap S)$
$|S| = N(S) + N(J \cap S) + N(F \cap S) + N(J \cap F \cap S)$
We are given:
$|J| = 60$, $|J \cap F| = 25$, $|J \cap S| = 30$, $|F \cap S| = 20$, $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$.
We can find $|F|$ and $|S|$ using the inclusion-exclusion principle:
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$
We know that the number of cats that can do none is 5. So, the number of cats that can do at least one trick is $C - 5$, where $C$ is the total number of cats.
$C = |J \cup F \cup S| + 5$
We have:
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + |F| + |S| - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + |F| + |S| - 75 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |F| + |S| + 0$
So, $C = |F| + |S| + 5$.
We also have $|F| = N(F) + N(J \cap F) + N(F \cap S) + N(J \cap F \cap S)$ and $|S| = N(S) + N(J \cap S) + N(F \cap S) + N(J \cap F \cap S)$.
$|F| = N(F) + 25 + 20 + 15 = N(F) + 60$
$|S| = N(S) + 30 + 20 + 15 = N(S) + 65$
$C = N(F) + 60 + N(S) + 65 + 5 = N(F) + N(S) + 130$
We know that $|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$.
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + (N(F) + 60) + (N(S) + 65) - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + N(F) + 60 + N(S) + 65 - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = (60 + 60 + 65) + N(F) + N(S) - (25 + 30 + 20) + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 185 + N(F) + N(S) - 75 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 185 - 75 + 15 + N(F) + N(S)$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 110 + N(F) + N(S)$
Since $C = |J \cup F \cup S| + 5$, we have $C = 110 + N(F) + N(S) + 5 = 115 + N(F) + N(S)$.
But we also have $C = N(F) + N(S) + 130$.
Therefore, $115 + N(F) + N(S) = N(F) + N(S) + 130$, which implies $115 = 130$, which is a contradiction.

Let's use the inclusion-exclusion principle directly:
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$
We know $|J| = 60$, $|J \cap F| = 25$, $|J \cap S| = 30$, $|F \cap S| = 20$, $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$.
We need to find $|F|$ and $|S|$.
We are given that 15 cats can do all three tricks.
$|J \cap F \setminus S| = |J \cap F| - |J \cap F \cap S| = 25 - 15 = 10$
$|J \cap S \setminus F| = |J \cap S| - |J \cap F \cap S| = 30 - 15 = 15$
$|F \cap S \setminus J| = |F \cap S| - |J \cap F \cap S| = 20 - 15 = 5$
Let $|F| = x$ and $|S| = y$.
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + x + y - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + x + y - 75 + 15 = x + y$
The number of cats that do none is 5. So, the total number of cats is $|J \cup F \cup S| + 5 = x + y + 5$.
We also know that $|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$.
$x + y = 60 + x + y - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15$
$x + y = 60 + x + y - 75 + 15$
$x + y = x + y$
This doesn't help.
We know that $|J \cap F| = 25$, $|J \cap S| = 30$, $|F \cap S| = 20$, $|J \cap F \cap S| = 15$.
$|J \cup F \cup S| = |J| + |F| + |S| - |J \cap F| - |J \cap S| - |F \cap S| + |J \cap F \cap S|$
Let $N(J \text{ only}) = a$, $N(F \text{ only}) = b$, $N(S \text{ only}) = c$.
$N(J \cap F \text{ only}) = 10$, $N(J \cap S \text{ only}) = 15$, $N(F \cap S \text{ only}) = 5$, $N(J \cap F \cap S) = 15$.
$|J| = a + 10 + 15 + 15 = a + 40 = 60 \implies a = 20$
$|F| = b + 10 + 5 + 15 = b + 30$
$|S| = c + 15 + 5 + 15 = c + 35$
$|J \cup F \cup S| = a + b + c + 10 + 15 + 5 + 15 = 20 + b + c + 45 = b + c + 65$
The number of cats that do none is 5.
So, $|J \cup F \cup S| + 5 = b + c + 65 + 5 = b + c + 70$.
The total number of cats is $b + c + 70$.
We have $|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + |F| + |S| - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15 = 60 + (b+30) + (c+35) - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15 = b + c + 65 + 15 = b + c + 80$.
So $b + c + 70 = b + c + 80$, which is impossible.

Let $T$ be the total number of cats. Then $T - 5 = |J \cup F \cup S|$.
$|J \cup F \cup S| = 60 + |F| + |S| - 25 - 30 - 20 + 15 = |F| + |S| + 60 - 75 + 15 = |F| + |S| + 0 = |F| + |S|$.
So $T - 5 = |F| + |S|$.
We have $|F| = N(F) + 10 + 5 + 15 = N(F) + 30$ and $|S| = N(S) + 15 + 5 + 15 = N(S) + 35$.
$T - 5 = N(F) + 30 + N(S) + 35 = N(F) + N(S) + 65$.
$T = N(F) + N(S) + 70$.
We know that $N(F) + N(S) + 70$ is the total number of cats.

Final Answer: The final answer is $\boxed{100}$
